chapters.title: Life can reroute you onto a path you never chose
chapters.text:
At twenty-two, Suleika Jaouad learned she had leukemia with a thirty-five percent survival estimate. For roughly fifteen hundred days she fought the disease supported by family and medical teams. Surviving was only the first hurdle — re-entering ordinary life while depleted proved equally daunting. Beating cancer did not end the war; it merely shifted the battlefield.

We assume life will deliver what we plan for, yet nothing equips us for the unforeseen.

Jaouad's ordeal shows that existence is not a controlled experiment. Circumstances can block dreams we once took for granted. Her story argues against despair in crisis — unexpected hardship can become raw material for rebuilding a meaningful life.

What started as an itch became a cancer diagnosis. She self-treated, assuming an insect bite. Fatigue followed — hours of sleep beyond normal. Weeks after graduating Princeton, doctors identified a rare acute myeloid leukemia.

Everyone who is born holds a dual citizenship in the kingdom of the well and the kingdom of the sick. ~ Susan Sontag

Jaouad attended Princeton on full scholarship, majoring in Near Eastern Studies with minors in French and Gender Studies. Multilingual from youth, she speaks French, English, Arabic, Spanish, and Farsi.

Daughter of a Tunisian-born literature professor and a Swiss-born painter, she recounts in later chapters her "incanceration" — chemotherapy rounds, a clinical trial, a bone marrow transplant — and names relearning how to live afterward as the hardest chapter of all.

chapters.title: Paris offered routine where New York had offered excess
chapters.text:
After Princeton, Jaouad followed classmates to New York in summer 2010, landing an unpaid internship at the Center for Constitutional Rights. Without family wealth, financial pressure mounted quickly.

An itch had begun months earlier; she blamed a parasite. During the internship it worsened, undermining work and daily life. She quit to seek paid employment — a decision that pulled her to Paris for a paralegal role.

Before departing she immersed herself in New York nightlife. Among many partners, Will stood out — a connection that might have deepened had she stayed.

Paris felt austere beside New York's intensity. She rented a private apartment on rue Dupetit-Thouars and entered corporate law for the first time. Within a week she knew paralegal work was not her calling.

A soul that needs to fly cannot thrive in solitude.

Correspondence with Will reignited her spirits. New friendships formed. Intimacy deepened until he visited for two weeks. They discovered shared journalistic ambitions, and by trip's end Will decided to move to Paris.

Joy is a terrifying emotion, don't trust it. ~ Suleika Jaouad

chapters.title: Few pleasures match doing what you love beside someone you love
chapters.text:
Previous relationships had ended despite Jaouad's full commitment, but Will was different — witty, playful, and thoughtful. He was twenty-seven; she was twenty-two. Everything seemed aligned.

Will enrolled in French classes and worked as a male nanny while awaiting a work visa. Meanwhile Jaouad's paralegal job grew unbearable as exhaustion deepened. She blamed the difficulty of adult life and searched for lighter work closer to her interests. A former journalism professor helped her secure a position at the International Herald Tribune — but before she could start, her health collapsed and she was flown back to New York.

Pursuing dreams requires a body that can keep up.

The itch had eased in Paris, yet fatigue intensified. A clinic visit for birth control revealed anemia on her blood panel — alarming but not yet urgent. She received contraceptives and an iron supplement.

Your appetite for knowing more, and knowing yourself better, makes you better. ~ Suleika Jaouad

chapters.title: Information becomes power when illness strikes
chapters.text:
Speculation swirled about her condition. Her father wondered whether HIV explained the symptoms given her sexual history. Biopsy results confirmed leukemia — finally accounting for the itch, exhaustion, and mouth sores. She withheld the news until she understood treatment options.

She called Will, stunned when he announced he was coming to her side. Word spread; offers of help poured in. They consulted a self-styled cancer guru without success. Jaouad scoured the internet. Full biopsy results brought a diagnosis of myelodysplastic syndrome from Dr. Holland at her New York hospital — a bone marrow disorder typically affecting patients over sixty, sometimes linked to toxins like benzene, pesticides, and lead.

Adversity tests the bond of love in more ways than one.

Will's presence during her darkest hour deepened her love. Doctors urged egg freezing or embryo creation before chemotherapy; she chose egg retrieval. The procedure triggered a painful urinary tract infection. Will remained steadfast, and they agreed to marry. His parents visited and offered support in their own way.

Did you know? Immunosuppressants help the body to ward off germs.

chapters.title: Channeling rage and frustration into creative momentum
chapters.text:
Jaouad's future clouded with terrifying unknowns. She mourned what illness was stealing — friendships, youth, the life she had imagined. Will's companionship provided her only anchor of normalcy. She urged him to find work, hoping stability would strengthen their bond.

Anger and frustration threatened to consume everything as dreams deferred piled up. Her parents, fearing depression, encouraged reconnection with old friends and social activity. A therapist proposed a "Hundred-Day Project" — daily creative work for one hundred days, each family member choosing their own pursuit. Will sent daily videos from the outside world. Her mother painted one ceramic tile per day, assembling a mosaic called "Suleika's Shield." Her father compiled one hundred one childhood memories into a book. Jaouad journaled. The project helped her move past rage, day by day.

During hardship, a creative ritual can lift mood and restore agency.

Doctors eventually said she needed a bone marrow transplant. Her brother proved a perfect match, though she grieved that he would endure pain on her behalf.

People treat you differently when you are facing the possibility of imminent death. ~ Suleika Jaouad

Did you know? Apheresis filters stem cells from blood plasma.

chapters.title: Finding purpose even inside isolation and fear
chapters.text:
Jaouad feared becoming another tragedy of unfulfilled promise. Confronting mortality, she resolved to make each day count. After a year in isolation she wanted an identity beyond "patient" and began blogging. Her first post, Good Afternoon, You Have Cancer, went viral. A follow-up, 10 Things Not To Say To A Cancer Patient, drew equal attention.

Write as if you were dying. We are all terminal patients on this earth — the mystery is not 'if' but 'when' death appears in the plotline. ~ Annie Dillard

A New York Times editor discovered the blog while Jaouad awaited final biopsy results before transplant. Despite no newspaper column experience, she pitched a video series alongside written pieces for readers too weak to read. From a hospital bed she negotiated the assignment and felt herself growing fierce. Life, Interrupted — column and video series — premiered March 29, 2012, days before transplant. She wrote about cancer, infertility, falling in love while falling ill, and facing death.

Imminent mortality can motivate feats otherwise unimaginable.

Transplant day became Day Zero. Patients typically wait one hundred days before doctors assess success — a period of semi-isolation. Extended hospitalization warps the psyche; claustrophobia sets in within weeks. Memory lapses complicated her new job. Reader mail became vital companionship. She coined "incanceration" after a death-row inmate compared solitary confinement to cancer isolation.

We were born needing care, and we will die needing care. ~ Suleika Jaouad

chapters.title: Rebuilding life after remission strains everyone involved
chapters.text:
On examination day Jaouad received mixed news. No cancer cells remained in her bone marrow — relief tempered by a high relapse risk from chromosomal abnormalities. Maintenance chemotherapy could stretch beyond a year.

The illness had exhausted everyone; normalcy felt overdue. Despondency settled in as Jaouad realized maintenance would be a solitary marathon.

First step: stability. She and Will moved into her mother's East Village apartment on Fourth Street and Avenue A.

Leaving hospitals for home demands more mental fortitude than inpatient treatment.

Will and Jaouad lacked that strength. Her parents returned to Saratoga; the medical team was no longer on call. Will absorbed caregiving roles alone.

Freedom meant struggling to keep food down, patience as Will fumbled new responsibilities, sharing a bed after months of hospital solitude, and relearning self-care.

Sticking to preventive treatment after remission is brutally hard when the cancer itself has vanished.

Jaouad rediscovered her love of rescuing stray animals, adopting a dog named Oscar. His presence brought joy that distracted from illness. Despite medical risks she refused to give him up.

Will felt his life had paused to care for her. The disease's sexual side effects strained their once passionate relationship until desire faded entirely. Tensions mounted until Will chose to step away.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Between Two Kingdoms chronicles the border between wellness and illness. Jaouad spent much of her adulthood in the kingdom of the sick and learned to build a life there. The kingdom of the well felt foreign and frightening. Fighting terminal disease did not exempt her from ordinary heartbreak, loss, and grief.

The reverse is also true: joy and connection exist inside illness. She met people who inspired and were inspired by her, landed meaningful work, adopted a dog, and experienced love. No one chooses sickness, yet everyone visits that kingdom at least once — preparation for the unexpected matters.

Crossing between realms is hard; hardest of all is re-entering wellness after prolonged illness. No treatment protocol or manual guides the transition. First you chase the old normal, then accept it is gone, then face a million possible new normals until exhaustion sets in. Ultimately you must explore boldly to discover what fits. You have survived too much to stop now. Claim the freedom to live fully.

Try this

Love the people around you without conditions — it is a foundation for happiness.

Seek reliable information rather than relying on casual opinions.

Reflect people's strengths back to them instead of adding to their burdens.

Read it till the end?
